map.condition: Need to count up to N distinct states with flip-flops
Memory capacity from address and data lines
Number of chips required for a given memory size

formulas.name: SR Latch (NAND) — Forbidden / Oscillation Condition
formulas.latex: S = 0,\; R = 0 \Rightarrow Q = Q' = 1 \text{ (both HIGH — forbidden/indeterminate)}
formulas.plain: S=0, R=0 in NAND SR latch => Q = Q' = 1 (forbidden state, may oscillate on return to 11)
formulas.whenToUse: When analysing NAND-based SR latch truth table and identifying the forbidden input pair
formulas.trap: For NAND SR, S=R=0 is forbidden (not S=R=1 as in NOR SR). Oscillation occurs if both go to 00 then simultaneously to 11.

formulas.name: SR Latch (NOR) — Forbidden Condition
formulas.latex: S = 1,\; R = 1 \Rightarrow Q = Q' = 0 \text{ (forbidden state)}
formulas.plain: S=1, R=1 in NOR SR latch => Q = Q' = 0 (forbidden state)
formulas.whenToUse: When analysing NOR-based SR latch and identifying which input pair causes indeterminate state
formulas.trap: NOR and NAND SR latches have opposite forbidden pairs; do not confuse them.

formulas.name: JK Flip-Flop Characteristic Equation
formulas.latex: Q_{n+1} = J\overline{Q_n} + \overline{K}Q_n
formulas.plain: Q(next) = J*NOT(Q) + NOT(K)*Q
formulas.whenToUse: To find the next state of a JK flip-flop given current state and inputs J, K
formulas.trap: When J=K=1, the flip-flop toggles (Q_{n+1} = NOT Q_n); this is the Toggle mode used in counters.

formulas.name: T (Toggle) Flip-Flop — JK Configured as T
formulas.latex: Q_{n+1} = T \oplus Q_n \quad (\text{i.e., } J = K = T)
formulas.plain: Q(next) = T XOR Q(current); achieved by tying J=K together
formulas.whenToUse: When a JK flip-flop has J=K=Q (feedback), confirming it acts as a T flip-flop toggling on every clock
formulas.trap: The output frequency of a single T flip-flop is f_clk / 2 (divides by 2 each stage).

formulas.name: D Flip-Flop Characteristic Equation
formulas.plain: Q(next) = D
formulas.whenToUse: When analysing D-type flip-flops; output simply follows D on active clock edge
formulas.trap: D flip-flop eliminates the forbidden SR state; commonly used in shift registers and pipeline stages.

formulas.name: Flip-Flops Required for N-State Counter
formulas.latex: n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
formulas.plain: n = ceil(log2(N))
formulas.whenToUse: To determine the minimum number of flip-flops needed to count N distinct states

terms.means: Number of states (modulus of the counter)

formulas.trap: A 3-bit counter can count up to 8 states max (0–7); a mod-5 counter still needs 3 flip-flops even though it only uses 5 of 8 states.

formulas.name: Counter Modulus (Count Sequence Length)
formulas.latex: \text{Modulus} = \text{number of distinct states in the counting sequence}
formulas.plain: Modulus = number of unique states the counter cycles through
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to determine the 'mod-N' nature of a given counter from its state table or timing diagram

terms.means: Total number of states before the counter repeats

formulas.trap: Always verify the actual sequence from the truth table; reset/skip logic can make modulus less than 2^n.

formulas.name: Ripple Counter Total Propagation Delay
formulas.latex: t_{pd,total} = n \times t_{pd}
formulas.plain: Total_delay = n * t_pd_per_flip_flop
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the maximum propagation delay of an asynchronous (ripple) counter with n stages

terms.means: Propagation delay of a single flip-flop

formulas.trap: In a synchronous counter all flip-flops are clocked together, so delay does NOT multiply with stages — only in ripple (asynchronous) counters.

formulas.name: Output Frequency after n Toggle Stages
formulas.latex: f_{out} = \frac{f_{clk}}{2^n}
formulas.plain: f_out = f_clk / 2^n
formulas.whenToUse: When n T flip-flops (or JK with J=K=1) are cascaded to form a frequency divider or ripple counter

terms.means: Number of cascaded T/JK flip-flop stages
Output frequency at the last stage

formulas.trap: Each stage divides by 2; for a mod-N counter the output period is N clock cycles, giving f_out = f_clk / N.

formulas.name: Mod-3 Counter / DFA State Count
formulas.latex: \text{Minimum states for mod-}k\text{ counter DFA} = k
formulas.plain: Minimum DFA states for a mod-k counter = k
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the minimum states in a DFA/FSM that counts 1s modulo k
formulas.trap: Each state represents a residue class {0,1,...,k-1}. For mod-3, exactly 3 states suffice.

formulas.name: Mealy vs Moore Machine — Output Dependency
formulas.latex: \text{Mealy: } \lambda(q,x) \quad \text{Moore: } \lambda(q)
formulas.plain: Mealy output depends on (state, input); Moore output depends on state only
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying a sequential machine or comparing the number of states between Mealy and Moore implementations
formulas.trap: A Mealy machine typically needs fewer states than an equivalent Moore machine. Moore output changes only on state transition, not immediately on input.

formulas.name: State Minimization — Distinguishability
formulas.latex: \text{States } p, q \text{ are equivalent iff } \forall\, x:\; \lambda(p,x) = \lambda(q,x) \;\wedge\; \delta(p,x) \equiv \delta(q,x)
formulas.plain: Two states are equivalent if for every input they produce the same output AND transition to equivalent next-states
formulas.whenToUse: When minimizing an FSM's state table using the table-filling (implication chart) method
formulas.trap: Start by separating states with different outputs; then iteratively check if next-state pairs are equivalent.

formulas.name: FSM Can Process Arbitrary-Length Sequences
formulas.latex: \text{FSM can add integers of arbitrary length } (\text{digit-by-digit, carry as state})
formulas.plain: An FSM can add two integers of any length by processing one digit at a time and encoding carry in a state
formulas.whenToUse: When asked if an FSM can handle unbounded / arbitrary-length arithmetic operations

terms.means: Stored as a finite state (0 or 1 for binary addition)

formulas.trap: The carry for binary addition has only 2 values, so it fits in a finite number of states — FSM CAN do it. This is TRUE.

formulas.name: Serial-In Serial-Out (SISO) Shift Register Delay
formulas.latex: \text{Delay} = n \text{ clock cycles for } n\text{-bit SISO register}
formulas.plain: An n-bit SISO register introduces a delay of n clock cycles
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the delay introduced by a SISO shift register used as a pipeline stage or delay element

terms.means: Number of flip-flop stages in the register

formulas.trap: Do not confuse with SIPO (serial-in parallel-out) where all n bits are available simultaneously after n clock cycles.

formulas.name: Ring Counter — Number of States
formulas.latex: \text{Ring counter with } n \text{ flip-flops has } n \text{ valid states}
formulas.plain: Ring counter valid states = n (not 2^n)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many states a ring (circulating) counter produces vs a Johnson counter
formulas.trap: Ring counter has n states; Johnson (twisted-ring) counter has 2n states. Both have far fewer than 2^n.

formulas.name: Johnson Counter — Number of States
formulas.latex: \text{Johnson counter with } n \text{ flip-flops has } 2n \text{ valid states}
formulas.plain: Johnson counter valid states = 2n
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the modulus of a Johnson (switch-tail ring) counter
formulas.trap: The Johnson counter produces a Gray-code-like sequence; it has self-correcting and non-self-correcting variants.

formulas.name: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Counter — Key Properties
formulas.latex: \text{Sync: all FFs clocked together} \Rightarrow \text{no cumulative delay}\\\text{Async (ripple): FFs chained} \Rightarrow t_{total} = n \times t_{pd}
formulas.plain: Synchronous: all flip-flops share one clock, no cumulative delay. Asynchronous/ripple: each FF clocks the next, delay = n*t_pd
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing counter types for speed, glitch-susceptibility, or delay analysis
formulas.trap: Ripple counters are slower and may show glitches between states; synchronous counters are faster but require more complex combinational logic.
formulas.reference: Digital Logic / Synchronous and Asynchronous Circuits

formulas.name: Maximum Clock Frequency of a Synchronous Circuit
formulas.latex: f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{setup} + t_{pd,combo} + t_{clk\text{-}Q}}
formulas.plain: f_max = 1 / (t_setup + t_pd_combinational + t_clk-to-Q)
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the maximum operating frequency of a synchronous sequential circuit (critical path timing)

terms.means: Combinational logic propagation delay on critical path

formulas.trap: Increase in combinational depth directly reduces f_max; pipelining reduces t_pd_combo per stage.

formulas.name: Address and Data Lines from Memory Specification
formulas.latex: \text{Memory } 2^k \times w:\; k \text{ address lines},\; w \text{ data lines}
formulas.plain: Memory of 2^k x w: k address lines, w data lines
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many address/data lines are needed for a memory of given capacity (e.g., 4K x 16)

terms.means: Word width = number of data lines

formulas.trap: 4K = 4 x 1024 = 2^12, so 12 address lines are needed for 4K x 16 (not 10 or 11).

formulas.name: Number of Chips Needed to Build Target Memory
formulas.latex: \text{Chips} = \frac{\text{Target capacity}}{\text{Single chip capacity}}
formulas.plain: Chips = Target_capacity / Chip_capacity (address expansion * word expansion)
formulas.whenToUse: When computing how many RAM/ROM chips are required to build a memory of a given size

terms.means: Total memory size required (in bits or bytes)
Size of each individual chip (in same units)

formulas.trap: First convert all sizes to the same unit (bits or bytes). For 4M bytes using 32K x 1 chips: 4M x 8 bits total / (32K x 1 bit per chip) = 1024 chips — check address expansion AND word expansion separately.

formulas.name: Chip Count — Address and Word Expansion
formulas.latex: \text{Chips} = \underbrace{\frac{\text{Target words}}{\text{Chip words}}}_{\text{address expansion}} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Target word width}}{\text{Chip word width}}}_{\text{word expansion}}
formulas.plain: Chips = (Target_words / Chip_words) * (Target_width / Chip_width)
formulas.whenToUse: For precise chip calculation when both address and word-width expansions are needed

terms.means: Number of addressable locations in the desired memory
Number of addressable locations per chip

formulas.trap: For 32K x 1 chips to build 4M x 8: address expansion = 4M/32K = 128, word expansion = 8/1 = 8, total = 128 x 8 = 1024 chips.

formulas.name: DRAM Refresh Overhead Percentage
formulas.latex: \% \text{ overhead} = \frac{r \times t_r}{T} \times 100
formulas.plain: % overhead = (refresh_rate * refresh_cycle_time / total_observation_period) * 100
formulas.whenToUse: When computing what fraction of memory cycle time or observation period is consumed by DRAM refresh operations

terms.means: Number of refreshes per unit time (refresh rate)
Time taken for each refresh cycle
Total observation period (same units as r * t_r)

formulas.trap: Units must be consistent. E.g., 100 refreshes/ms x 100 ns each = 10000 ns = 10 µs per ms = 1% overhead — not 6.4% or 10%.

formulas.name: Memory Capacity Formula
formulas.latex: \text{Capacity} = 2^k \times w \text{ (bits)}
formulas.plain: Capacity (bits) = 2^k * w, where k = address bits, w = word width
formulas.whenToUse: To compute the total storage capacity given address bus and data bus widths

terms.means: Word width in bits (data bus width)

formulas.trap: Divide by 8 to convert bits to bytes. A 12-bit address x 16-bit data bus = 2^12 x 16 = 65536 bytes = 64 KB (after /8 per word and x4096 words).
